drafts.draft: The thing nobody tells small businesses about WhatsApp bots: 90% of the win is just answering at 11pm, not the fancy intent classification.
drafts.reason: SMB pain angle. Concrete time hook (11pm) + contrarian framing (boring beats clever).

drafts.draft: If you build for tradies you learn quick: the customer doesn't want chat, they want a callback time. Anything else feels like a maze. Bots that answer 'when' beat bots that answer 'what'.
drafts.reason: Trade-specific insight. Pattern that converts on /pricing.html. Reply-bait: tradies will share their own version.

drafts.draft: We rebuilt our outreach copy three times. The version that works is shorter than the failed ones, every time. AI didn't help us write it. AI helped us cut it.
drafts.reason: Counter-AI-hype angle. Builders will reply with their own AI editing experience.

drafts.target_text: An early Claude Mythos Preview snapshot we provided METR has a time horizon of more than 2x the next best model on their 80% success rate benchmark
drafts.draft: What workload class shows the biggest gap? We're seeing the 80% bar bend most on multi-step ops where the model has to undo its own mistakes. Curious where METR's 2x is most pronounced.
drafts.reason: Anthropic principal. Direct question to author (reply-back = 150x). 79k views, 6.2h.

drafts.draft: Which signal are you watching first? Daily active or session length? We're tracking session length on our SMB setup and it's the more honest one when novelty wears off.
drafts.reason: Perplexity CEO. Cryptic OP invites question. 496k followers, fresh.

drafts.target_text: ~1,000 km later. Unsolicited plug: continues to be the best hack for motivating me 'Eh, come on, why not one more mile.' on my morning constitutional.
drafts.draft: Curious which gear that is. The 'one more mile' bargain is real, I have it with the dog at 6am. What got you to 1000 vs giving up at 200?
drafts.reason: Patrick McKenzie. Personal hook + question that begs the why. SaaS audience.

drafts.target_text: 200+ speakers coming to SaaStr AI Annual. The prior years, an (often grouchy) human spent hours sending the calendar invites, got 5%+ wrong. Last night, Claude Cowork just did it all.
drafts.draft: Was the human reading the wrong fields, or did the calendar provider keep mangling timezones? Curious if Claude beat the human on accuracy or just on patience.
drafts.reason: SaaStr founder. AI agent use case adjacent to our space. Forces a real answer.

drafts.target_text: 13 years of SaaStr. Hundreds of events. Tens of thousands of founders helped. SaaStr AI Annual 2026 is the most important one we've ever done. May 12-14.
drafts.draft: What's the one talk you're most nervous won't land? The 'most important one ever' framing usually means there's a session that has to convert and you're sweating it.
drafts.reason: Same OP. Pulls a genuine confession out of him if he replies. Both jasonlk replies will be fine if both posted (different threads).

drafts.target_text: You can only keep one for the next 5 years: audience, product, skills, network
drafts.draft: Audience. Product is a snapshot, audience compounds. I've tried to rebuild from scratch twice and skill comes back fast, audience does not. What's your gut answer?
drafts.reason: Browser source. Pure conversation starter, OP will pick a winner. Low risk of skip.

drafts.target_text: The 'agentic AI' pitch right now reminds me of RPA in 2018. Same promise: it works end-to-end, no humans needed. Same reality: brittle pipelines, silent failures, and someone on a Slack channel handling exceptions.
drafts.draft: This is the cleanest version of that take I've read. The closest analog is also the bots-vs-RPA gap of who owns the failure mode. Who do your clients call when the agent silently drops a spec?
drafts.reason: Browser source. Directly on our positioning thesis (we are the human in the failure-mode loop). Small account but right audience.
